planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring or fall in any well-drained soil in shade; Persian ivy establishes quickly and is less prone to the leaf spot and spider mite problems that affect English ivy in hot, humid summers of the South.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full to partial shade — Persian ivy is more tolerant of deep shade than English ivy and produces dramatically larger leaves, up to 8–10 inches across, with a bold, tropical quality that makes it stand apart as a groundcover or climbing vine in shaded areas. The dark, leathery, heart-shaped leaves have a glossy surface and a pleasant resinous fragrance when handled. Unlike the widely planted English ivy (H. helix), Persian ivy is far less invasive and rarely escapes into natural areas in North America. The cultivar 'Sulphur Heart' features leaves with bold yellow centers and dark green edges, making it one of the most striking shade groundcovers available; 'Dentata Variegata' has cream-margined leaves. Berries are toxic — do not plant where children or pets may ingest them. Excellent on north-facing slopes and under dense tree canopies.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring or fall in any reasonably well-drained soil in shade to partial shade; Atlantic ivy establishes rapidly and provides dense weed-suppressing cover within 1–2 seasons. Do not plant near natural areas in the Pacific Northwest or parts of the Southeast where it is invasive.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average, moist soil in shade to partial shade; Atlantic ivy is closely related to English ivy (H. helix) and often sold mislabeled as such, but it is botanically distinct with larger, broader, 5-lobed leaves on more robust stems. It forms a vigorous, dense evergreen groundcover that suppresses weeds effectively and tolerates heavy shade under mature trees. In humid, mild climates of zones 7–10 it grows especially vigorously; in the Pacific Northwest and parts of the Southeast it can escape cultivation into natural areas and should not be planted near wildlands. The small, nectar-rich flowers in fall are an important late-season food source for ivy bees (Colletes hederae) and other insects. Berries are mildly toxic; all parts of the plant can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.

planting.note: Plant divisions of the rhizomatous rootstock in fall or early spring in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in deep shade; redwood sorrel requires cool, moist conditions and will not survive in dry or exposed sites.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in zones 7–9; redwood sorrel is native to the floor of coastal conifer forests from the California redwoods north to British Columbia, where it forms dense, lush carpets of clover-like trifoliate leaves in one of the deepest shade environments in North America. The leaves close and fold downward in bright light — a nyctinastic response also seen in clover — and reopen in shade or at night. Small white to pale pink flowers bloom from spring through early summer. The foliage is edible in small quantities as a salad ingredient or garnish, with a bright, lemony flavor from oxalic acid; consume in moderation as large quantities can interfere with calcium absorption. Provides one of the best groundcovers for deep, moist shade under mature conifers or where dense tree canopy suppresses most other plants.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, acidic, lean soil (pH 4.5–5.5) in partial shade; dryland blueberry is one of the few blueberry species that tolerates dry shade and lean, rocky, or sandy acidic woodland conditions where standard highbush blueberry would fail.

directions: Plant in dry to well-drained, acidic, sandy or rocky soil in partial shade or dappled woodland light; dryland blueberry is a compact native Vaccinium of eastern US woodlands and acidic ridge tops that earns its common name from its exceptional tolerance of dry, lean, acidic conditions — it is among the most drought-tolerant blueberries in cultivation. The white to pink bell-shaped flowers in spring are followed by small, intensely sweet blue-black berries in midsummer that are prized for fresh eating and bird fodder. Excellent fall color in shades of orange, red, and burgundy. Unlike highbush cultivars, no irrigation or supplemental fertilizer is needed once established in appropriate soil. Plant several individuals for cross-pollination and heavier berry crops. An underused native for dry, shaded slopes and woodland gardens.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; cow parsnip is monocarpic (blooms once then dies) but self-seeds freely to maintain a colony. Seeds must be fresh — viability drops sharply after several months. CAUTION: The sap causes severe phototoxic burns (photodermatitis) when skin exposure is followed by sunlight. Always wear gloves when handling plants.

directions: Grow in moist to average, humus-rich soil in partial shade or at woodland edges and stream margins; cow parsnip is the largest native North American member of the carrot family, with enormous palmate leaves and flat-topped white flower umbels up to 12 inches across that serve as insect landing pads for hundreds of pollinator species. The young peeled shoots and leaf stems have been eaten by Indigenous peoples across North America — cooked, the flavor resembles a mild celery-like vegetable. IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE: The sap of cow parsnip contains furanocoumarins that cause severe photodermatitis — blisters and burns when sap-exposed skin is hit by sunlight. Always wear gloves and long sleeves when harvesting or handling, and wash hands thoroughly afterward. Do not confuse with the invasive and more toxic giant hogweed (H. mantegazzianum), which is larger with a purple-blotched stem.
